title: New York Fed Launches Blog and Economic Policy Review Series Examining the Evolution of Banking

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Liberty Street Economics blog announced a seven-part series (July 16, 2012) examining banks' changing role in financial intermediation, accompanied by Economic Policy Review articles. The series explores credit intermediation chains, securitization, and the growing participation of nonbank entities in credit markets. An overarching theme is that banks remain major players in modern credit intermediation despite structural changes.
key_points: NY Fed announces a seven-part Liberty Street Economics blog series on banking evolution, published July 16, 2012.
Series co-published with Economic Policy Review articles exploring the credit intermediation chain associated with securitization.
Topics include banks' use of the originate-to-distribute model, banks as credit enhancers, and their roles as issuers, underwriters, servicers, and trustees.
Series examines size, organizational complexity, and scope of large bank holding companies.
Series explores banks' continued importance as central intermediaries for monitoring innovations and risk in the 'shadow' banking system.
Views expressed are those of the authors and do not necessarily reflect the position of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York or the Federal Reserve System.
why_it_matters: This 2012 series represents a historical New York Fed research initiative examining how banks adapt to financial innovation and shadow banking risks. While dated, it captures early post-crisis Fed thinking on credit intermediation, nonbank participation in lending, and regulatory monitoring implications. The series provided a framework for understanding the structural evolution of banking that informed subsequent regulatory approaches. Note: This source is an announcement/preview of future blog content rather than substantive research findings.
market_structure_context: Series directly examines financial intermediation structure: the credit chain from origination through distribution, the role of securitization, bank holding company complexity, and the relationship between banks and shadow banking entities. Key structural concepts include originate-to-distribute models and banks as credit enhancers along the intermediation chain.
